title: Brooklyn Borough Hall

infobox.value: is a building in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), New York City. It was designed by architects [Calvin Pollard](/wiki/Calvin_Pollard "Calvin Pollard") and [Gamaliel King](/wiki/Gamaliel_King "Gamaliel King") in the [Greek Revival style](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture"), and constructed of [Tuckahoe marble](/wiki/Tuckahoe_marble "Tuckahoe marble") under the supervision of superintendent [Stephen Haynes](/wiki/Stephen_Haynes "Stephen Haynes").

**Brooklyn Borough Hall** is a building in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), New York City. It was designed by architects [Calvin Pollard](/wiki/Calvin_Pollard "Calvin Pollard") and [Gamaliel King](/wiki/Gamaliel_King "Gamaliel King") in the [Greek Revival style](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture"), and constructed of [Tuckahoe marble](/wiki/Tuckahoe_marble "Tuckahoe marble") under the supervision of superintendent [Stephen Haynes](/wiki/Stephen_Haynes "Stephen Haynes").

It was completed in 1848 as the [City Hall](/wiki/City_Hall "City Hall") for the [City of Brooklyn](/wiki/History_of_Brooklyn "History of Brooklyn"). In January 1898 the independent City of Brooklyn merged with the [City of New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York"), and Kings County became the [Borough](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), at which time the building became Brooklyn Borough Hall.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Borough_Hall&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Borough_Hall&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Construction")]

In 1834, the year Brooklyn was granted its [city charter](/wiki/City_charter "City charter"), the land for Brooklyn's [city hall](/wiki/City_hall "City hall") was donated by the Remsen and Pierrepont families, whose names are commemorated in the names of Remsen and Pierrepont Streets in nearby [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights "Brooklyn Heights"). The following year, New York architect [Calvin Pollard](/wiki/Calvin_Pollard "Calvin Pollard") won the commission to design the building in a contest held by the city. The foundations were dug and the cornerstone laid for this structure in 1836. However, financial hardship halted construction entirely.[[2]](#cite_note-inside-2)

When funds again became available in 1845 construction resumed, this time of a structure designed by [Gamaliel King](/wiki/Gamaliel_King "Gamaliel King"), who had come in second to Pollard in the city's design competition, with instructions from the city that the new building must fit inside the already laid foundation.[[2]](#cite_note-inside-2) King preserved many elements of Pollard's original design and intent, including its [Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture") style, although the project was scaled down in size somewhat.[[3]](#cite_note-agb-3) Construction was completed in 1848.

### Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Borough_Hall&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Usage")]

The Kings County Courthouse was built in 1868, turning this area – now known as [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") – into a government center and busy area of commerce. In the 1940s, the Kings County Courthouse and other nearby buildings to the north were replaced by a complex of courthouses and a plaza in front of Borough Hall. A mall connects the building to [Cadman Plaza Park](/wiki/Cadman_Plaza_Park "Cadman Plaza Park").[[4]](#cite_note-nrhpinv_ny-4)

On February 26, 1895, waste paper caught fire and destroyed the [cupola](/wiki/Cupola "Cupola") and the statue of Justice that stood atop it,[[5]](#cite_note-nyt-5) as well as the top floors of the building; water damage ruined the walls and ceiling of the Common Council chamber. Three years later, a new [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") [cast-iron](/wiki/Cast-iron "Cast-iron") cupola was built, designed by Vincent C. Griffith and the firm of [Stoughton and Stoughton](/wiki/Stoughton_and_Stoughton "Stoughton and Stoughton"),[[3]](#cite_note-agb-3)[[6]](#cite_note-aia-6)[[7]](#cite_note-nycland-7) on which was placed a flag.[[4]](#cite_note-nrhpinv_ny-4) In 1898, the city of Brooklyn was [consolidated](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1898%E2%80%931945) "History of New York City (1898–1945)") into the [five boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)") of New York City, and this building ceased being "City Hall" and became "Borough Hall". In 1902, the Common Council room was demolished to build a new courtroom, designed in the [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") style by Brooklyn architect [Axel Hedman](/w/index.php?title=Axel_Hedman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Axel Hedman (page does not exist)").

Starting in the 1930s, there were numerous proposals to raze Borough Hall, based on arguments that it no longer performed any government function, that its architecture was not particularly notable, and that it was a monument to an extremely brief era in Brooklyn's history. In 1966, the building was designated a [city landmark](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Landmarks "List of New York City Landmarks") by the then-new [New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks_Preservation_Commission "New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission"). The building was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/List_of_Registered_Historic_Places_in_Kings_County,_New_York "List of Registered Historic Places in Kings County, New York") on January 10, 1980.

In the 1980s, the building underwent a massive renovation under the supervision of [Conklin & Rossant](/wiki/Conklin_%26_Rossant "Conklin & Rossant"). The original copper shingling on the cupola was restored by Les metalliers Champenois, the same metalworks involved in the [restoration of the Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Restoration_of_the_Statue_of_Liberty_1984-1986 "Restoration of the Statue of Liberty 1984-1986"), and the flag on the cupola was replaced by a new figure of [Lady Justice](/wiki/Lady_Justice "Lady Justice").[[4]](#cite_note-nrhpinv_ny-4) The renovation was completed in 1989. In the mid-2010s, the plaza became a popular [skate spot](/wiki/Skate_spot "Skate spot") after a restoration.[[6]](#cite_note-aia-6)[[7]](#cite_note-nycland-7)
